title: Section 1: Neurones — The Messengers
content: In this section, we'll look at the cells that carry messages through your nervous system.

title: What Is the Nervous System?
content: The nervous system is the body's communication network.
bullets: Detects changes in your environment
Sends electrical signals through the body
Coordinates your body's responses
image: Simple body outline showing brain, spinal cord, and branching nerves

title: Two Main Divisions
content: The nervous system is divided into two parts:
bullets: Central Nervous System (CNS) — brain and spinal cord
Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) — all other nerves
image: Diagram: body with CNS highlighted in blue, PNS nerves branching out in green

title: What Are Neurones?
content: Neurones are specialised nerve cells that transmit electrical impulses.
bullets: They carry messages as electrical signals
They connect the CNS to the rest of the body
There are three types of neurones
image: Simple neurone shape with 'electrical signal' arrow

title: Type 1: Sensory Neurones
content: Sensory neurones carry signals FROM sense organs TO the CNS.
bullets: Detect stimuli (heat, light, sound, touch)
Send information to the brain or spinal cord
Example: touching a hot pot sends a signal to your brain
image: Labelled diagram of a sensory neurone: cell body, dendron, axon, myelin sheath

title: Type 2: Motor Neurones
content: Motor neurones carry signals FROM the CNS TO muscles or glands.
bullets: They tell your body how to respond
Signal goes from brain/spinal cord to effectors
Effectors are muscles or glands that carry out the response
image: Labelled diagram of a motor neurone: cell body, dendrites, long axon, motor end plate

title: Type 3: Relay Neurones
content: Relay neurones connect sensory and motor neurones inside the CNS.
bullets: Found in the brain and spinal cord only
Act as the 'middle man' between sensory and motor neurones
Also called interneurones
image: Diagram showing relay neurone connecting sensory neurone to motor neurone within the spinal cord

title: Think About It 🤔
content: When you accidentally step on a sharp object, which type of neurone detects the pain first?
bullets: Hint: which type carries signals TO the CNS?

title: Structure of a Neurone
content: All neurones share some common features:
bullets: Cell body — contains the nucleus
Dendrites — receive signals from other neurones
Axon — long fibre that carries the impulse away
Myelin sheath — insulates the axon, speeds up signals
image: Detailed labelled neurone diagram: cell body, nucleus, dendrites, axon, myelin sheath, axon terminal

title: How Signals Travel
content: Nerve impulses travel as electrical signals along the neurone.
bullets: Impulse moves from dendrites → cell body → axon
Myelin sheath makes the signal travel faster
At the end, the signal passes to the next neurone or effector
image: Animation-style diagram: signal moving along a neurone with directional arrows

title: Did You Know? ⚡
content: Nerve impulses can travel up to 120 metres per second in myelinated neurones.
bullets: That's faster than the fastest car in Zambia on the highway
Without myelin, signals slow down dramatically
Diseases like Multiple Sclerosis damage the myelin sheath
image: Speed comparison infographic: nerve impulse vs car vs running

title: Comparing the Three Neurones
content: Here's how sensory, motor, and relay neurones differ:
bullets: Sensory: long dendron, cell body on the side
Motor: cell body at one end, long axon
Relay: short, found only in the CNS
image: Side-by-side comparison diagram of all three neurone types with labels

title: Section 1 Summary
bullets: The nervous system = CNS + PNS
Neurones carry electrical impulses
3 types: sensory, motor, relay
Key parts: cell body, dendrites, axon, myelin sheath
